title: Interpreting AI output variation — what it tells you about reliability
verdict: PASS — all hard checks clear; 1 soft finding (bold+defining-clause on prior term 'ground truth' at line 66, below escalation threshold).

format_discipline.notes: First non-empty line is H1 title; sidecar present and parses; all six fixed sections present in correct order.

length.notes: 1047 words is within 800–1200 range; 5.24 min at 200 wpm is within 4–6 min range.

citations.notes: All three resources cited inline; bibliography entries match markers; no orphans; >60% of key claims carry markers.

findings.description: Line 66 uses the bold+defining-clause shape '**ground truth** — the confirmed correct answer used to evaluate model outputs' for a term introduced at topic 7.10. Per the known-pattern rule, deferred or prior concepts should use bare name only (or a plain parenthetical), not the bold+definition shape. The content itself is a valid one-sentence reminder appropriate for a capstone topic, but the formatting pattern should be: 'ground truth (the confirmed correct answer from topic 7.10)' or simply 'ground truth'.

prerequisite_alignment.notes: Topics 7.4 and 7.5 referenced correctly as prior knowledge without re-teaching mechanics. Ground truth (7.10) has a soft formatting violation; no re-teaching of any prior concept's mechanism.

forward_reference_discipline.notes: All taught concepts are within the declared scope for 7.11. No advanced adjacent concepts (F1 score, ROC curves, calibration, etc.) introduced.

sampled_assertions.assertion: Output variation is the measurable spread across AI answers to the same prompt across multiple runs
When probability distribution has strong mass on one answer, repeated sampling almost always lands in same place = low variation
You cannot interpret variation without knowing the temperature setting
Low temperature + high variation = reliability warning; high temperature + high variation = expected
Four quadrants: Q1 = strongest confidence, Q2 = reliability warning, Q3 = surprisingly robust, Q4 = expected for open-ended
Deterministic tasks — one correct answer; examples: factual recall, yes/no classification, medical symptom checking
A model can be confidently wrong — same incorrect answer every time with zero variation
Worked example: 10 runs at T=0, Yes×6 No×4, correct=Yes, Q2 reliability warning, 60% accuracy
Ground truth checking is the only way to know whether consistent output is also correct

beginner_readability.notes: All non-everyday terms defined at first use (LLM, output variation, temperature-adjusted variation reading, ground truth, deterministic task, open-ended task). Opening with a concrete 10-runs scenario before abstraction. Sentence length well under 25 words average. No hedging phrases detected.

formatting.notes: No paragraph exceeds ~4 sentences. Worked Example uses a numbered list. In Practice uses a bullet checklist and a table. Inline examples present in Key Concepts (temperature scenario) and in deterministic/open-ended task paragraphs. Definitions use bold-term / plain-meaning shape consistently.

assessment_references.notes: Line 104 references 'the week 7 lab' as a learning activity description, not an assessment ID or grade weight. No A-code, due week, or percentage found.

scope_creep.notes: All concepts taught are within the 7.11 scope boundary. Prior-week topics (accuracy, precision, recall, confusion matrix) are named and pointed to, not re-taught.

diagram_embed.notes: Diagram embed present at line 35 with descriptive alt text and a caption.

soft_findings.description: **ground truth** — definition shape used for a term owned by topic 7.10. Use bare name or plain parenthetical instead of bold+defining-clause for prior concepts.
soft_findings.suggested_fix: Change '**ground truth** — the confirmed correct answer used to evaluate model outputs' to 'ground truth (the confirmed correct answer you met in topic 7.10)' or simply reference 'ground truth' without the defining clause.

escalation_note: Soft finding count 1 < threshold 3; no single scope_creep finding; no escalation to hard fail.
